questions.q: JWT access token. Why should TTL be short (15 min)?
choices: JWTs are stateless — hard to revoke until expiry; short TTL limits blast radius of theft
questions.explanation: Stateless JWTs can't be invalidated mid-TTL without a server-side revocation list. Short TTL = less exposure if token leaks. Pair with long-lived refresh tokens stored securely.

questions.q: OAuth 2.0: which grant type is RECOMMENDED for mobile apps / single-page apps today?
questions.explanation: Authorization code + PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange): secure for public clients without client_secret. Implicit grant is deprecated. Password grant is an anti-pattern.

questions.q: Storing passwords: what's the correct approach?
choices: Salt + bcrypt/scrypt/argon2 with cost tuned for ~100ms
questions.explanation: Bcrypt/Argon2 with per-user salt + tunable cost. Slow by design — defeats brute force. MD5/SHA-256 are fast and GPU-crackable. Never store plaintext even in 'encrypted DB'.

questions.q: Defense against DDoS at L7 (application) — what's the first line of defense?
choices: CDN with WAF + rate limiting + bot mitigation
questions.explanation: CDN (Cloudflare/Akamai) absorbs volumetric attacks at edge. WAF filters L7 application-layer. Rate limits per IP / ASN / bot-fingerprint. Your origin never sees the flood.

questions.q: Zero-trust networking vs perimeter security. Key principle?
choices: Never trust — authenticate + authorize every request regardless of network origin
questions.explanation: Zero-trust: 'never trust, always verify.' Requests from internal networks are authenticated same as external. Assumes breach — limits blast radius. Google BeyondCorp pioneered this.

questions.q: Secret rotation — why does frequent (e.g., daily) rotation help?
choices: If a secret leaks, it's invalid soon; shrinks window of useful-to-attacker time
questions.explanation: Leaked credentials are a fact of life. Rotation shortens exploitability. Automated rotation with grace windows (old + new both valid for N hours) prevents downtime.

questions.q: Rate-limit on login endpoint. Fail which way?
questions.explanation: Login is a brute-force target. Fail-open = free path for attackers during outage. Fail-closed = temporary legit-user pain vs credential stuffing. Security > availability on auth paths.

questions.q: An attacker steals a user's JWT access token (15-min TTL) from a compromised browser extension. The token is stateless and your backend has no revocation list. What can the attacker do and for how long?
choices: Full access to the user's account for up to 15 minutes until the token expires; you cannot revoke it without server-side changes
questions.explanation: Stateless JWTs cannot be revoked mid-TTL without adding a server-side deny-list (which defeats the stateless advantage). The attacker has full access until expiry. Mitigations: short TTL (5-15 min), token binding to IP/fingerprint, server-side revocation list for critical apps, and immediate refresh-token rotation on suspected compromise.

questions.q: Your SPA at app.example.com makes API calls to api.example.com. A developer sets CORS to Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. What security hole does this create?
choices: Any website can make credentialed requests to your API on behalf of logged-in users, enabling CSRF-like attacks
questions.explanation: Allow-Origin: * with Allow-Credentials: true means ANY origin can send requests with the user's cookies/tokens. An attacker's site (evil.com) can make authenticated API calls as the user. Fix: explicitly whitelist allowed origins (app.example.com) and never combine wildcard with credentials. Browsers actually block this specific combo, but misconfigurations with dynamic origin reflection create the same vulnerability.

questions.q: You rotate your database password on a Saturday. The new password is updated in AWS Secrets Manager, but the application pods still have the old password cached from startup. Deploys happen Monday. What breaks?
choices: When connection pools recycle or pods restart, they fetch the old cached password and fail to connect to the database
The database rejects all connections immediately
questions.explanation: Secret rotation without consumer coordination creates a gap. Best practice: applications should poll Secrets Manager periodically (or use a sidecar that watches for changes), support dual-credential windows (old + new both valid during rotation), and test rotation in staging. AWS Secrets Manager rotation lambdas can coordinate this automatically.

questions.q: Your API accepts user-provided URLs for webhook callbacks. An attacker submits http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ as the webhook URL. What attack is this?
choices: SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery): your server fetches the AWS metadata endpoint, leaking IAM credentials to the attacker
questions.explanation: SSRF exploits the server's ability to make outbound requests. The AWS instance metadata endpoint (169.254.169.254) returns IAM credentials, which the attacker receives via the webhook response. Mitigations: block private/link-local IPs in outbound requests, use IMDSv2 (requires PUT with hop-limit), and run webhooks from an isolated network without metadata access.

questions.q: Your team stores API keys in a Git repository's .env file. Someone accidentally pushes it to a public GitHub repo. They force-push to remove the commit 5 minutes later. Are the secrets safe?
choices: No — GitHub events, forks, caches, and automated scanners (like TruffleHog, GitGuardian) likely captured the secrets within seconds; rotate immediately
Only if no one saw it
questions.explanation: Git force-push doesn't delete the commit object (it's still reachable by SHA for a while). GitHub's event API, any forks, RSS feeds, and automated secret-scanning bots index public commits in real-time. Assume the secrets are compromised the moment they hit a public remote. Rotate ALL exposed credentials immediately.
